answers.question: How do I build a city?
answers.response:
Catan (5th Ed.) — Building a City

- You don’t place a city from scratch; you upgrade one of your own settlements. (Almanac: Cities, p.6)
- When: During your Build phase on your turn, after rolling and any trades. (Game Rules: Turn Overview/Build, p.4)
- Cost: 3 Ore + 2 Grain. (Game Rules: c) City, p.5; Building Costs card)
- How: Pay the cost, take the chosen settlement off the board, and replace it with a city on the same intersection. Return the removed settlement to your personal supply. (Game Rules: c) City, p.5; Almanac: Cities, p.6)
- Effects: 
  - The city is worth 2 victory points. 
  - It produces double from each adjacent hex (2 resource cards when its number is rolled). (Game Rules: c) City, p.5; Almanac: Cities, p.6)
- Limits/notes: You can never have more than 4 cities (piece limit), and you must have a settlement on the board to upgrade. The Distance Rule doesn’t apply because you’re replacing an existing settlement. (Game Rules: Build limits, p.4; Almanac: Cities, p.6)

answers.question: What happens when I run out of roads?
answers.response:
You can’t build any more roads. 

- Official rule: “You cannot build more pieces than what is available in your pool—a maximum of … 15 roads” (Game Rules, p. 4).  
- Built roads stay on the board and are never returned to your supply (Almanac: Build, p. 6; Roads, p. 11). So once your 15 roads are out, you’re capped and cannot add new road segments even if you can pay for them.  
- Development card interaction: Road Building (“Place 2 new roads as if you had just built them,” Almanac: Progress Cards, p. 10) uses roads from your supply. Commonly accepted ruling/FAQ: if you have only 1 road left you may place 1; with 0, the card has no effect—so you’d typically wait to play it until you have pieces available.

answers.question: If I have multiple VP development cards, can I play them all in the same turn?
answers.response:
Yes. You may reveal any number of Victory Point (VP) development cards on your turn, and the “one development card per turn” limit does not apply to VP cards.

- Official rules: Game Rules p.5, “Victory Point Cards (Orange Frame)”: “You may play any number of victory point cards during your turn, even during the turn you purchase them.” Also notes you reveal them on your turn when you have 10 VPs.
- Almanac p.7 “Development Cards” (exception for VP cards) and p.14 “Victory Point Cards” reiterate this and that you win only on your own turn.

So you can flip multiple VP cards at once on your turn (including the turn you bought them) to reach 10 VP and win.

However, it is very verbose, repeating information and including rule citations which, while helpful for verification, make it less concise for a quick mid-game rule check compared to the gold answer's single, clear sentence.

answers.question: How can the Robber get activated?
answers.response:
The Robber is activated in two ways:
- Roll a 7 during the resource production roll. Then players with more than 7 cards discard half, you must move the Robber to another hex (or the desert), and you steal 1 random resource from an adjacent player (Game Rules, p.5, “4a. Rolling a ‘7’ and Activating the Robber”).
- Play a Knight development card. When played, you immediately move the Robber and steal 1 random resource from an adjacent player, same as on a 7 (Game Rules, p.5 “Knight Cards”; Almanac: Knight Cards, p.8; Robber, p.11).

answers.question: When does the game end?
answers.response: Official rule: The game ends immediately when a player has 10 or more victory points on their own turn and declares it (revealing any VP development cards as needed). If you reach 10 points when it is not your turn, play continues until a player (possibly you) has 10+ points on their turn. [Game Rules, “Ending the Game,” p.5; Almanac, “Ending the Game,” p.7; “Victory Points,” p.14]

answers.question: Can I "give away" resources with the following trade scheme? Player A trades 2 sheep for 1 wheat with player B. Then, Player A trades 1 wheat for 1 sheep with player B.
answers.response:
Short answer: Yes—under the base Catan rules this sequence is legal, as long as both trades happen on the active player’s turn.

Why:
- Domestic Trade (Almanac, p. 7) lets the current player make any number of trades and negotiate any terms with others. Each of your two exchanges is a valid trade (2 sheep for 1 wheat; then 1 wheat for 1 sheep). The rule only forbids “giving away cards” and “trading like resources” (e.g., 2 wool for 1 wool) in a single trade. Here, each individual trade is an exchange of different resources, so it’s allowed.
- Also remember only the player whose turn it is may trade; other players may not trade among themselves (Almanac, p. 7; Trade, p. 14). So both trades must occur on Player A’s turn.

Ambiguity/Community practice:
- Many groups house‑rule against “two‑step gifting” or “dumping” because it effectively transfers a resource with little or no compensation. Some organized play/tournament rules also forbid trades designed solely to gift resources or collude. If you’re playing competitively, check the event’s rules.

Bottom line:
- Official rules: Allowed if done during the active player’s turn and each trade is itself legal.
- Common house rule/tournament rule: Disallowed as “effective gifting” or collusion—agree at the table beforehand.

scores.reasoning: The generated answer is perfectly accurate, matching the gold standard on the legality and the effective outcome. It is also more complete, providing excellent rule-based reasoning and practical advice regarding house rules and tournament play. While longer than the gold answer, its structure and additional detail are valuable and not overly verbose.

answers.question: How many VPs would this be: 4 settlements, 1 city and longest road?
answers.response:
8 victory points.

- Settlements: 1 VP each → 4 settlements = 4 VP (Almanac “Victory Points,” p.14).
- City: 2 VP → 1 city = 2 VP (Almanac “Cities,” p.6; and “Victory Points,” p.14).
- Longest Road: 2 VP (Almanac “Longest Road,” p.9; “Victory Points,” p.14).

Total: 4 + 2 + 2 = 8 VP. Note a city replaces a settlement, so “4 settlements and 1 city” means four settlements plus one upgraded site.

However, it is less concise than the gold answer due to repeating the total VP and including citations, which might be excessive for a quick mid-game rule check.

answers.question: How are ties broken if multiple people reach 10 VPs at the same time?
answers.response:
There is no tie-breaker—only the player whose turn it is can win. If several players would be at 10+ VPs, the one who reaches 10 (or more) on their own turn wins immediately; others must wait until their turns to claim victory.

Official rules:
- Game Rules, Ending the Game (p.5): “If you have 10 or more victory points during your turn, the game ends and you are the winner! If you reach 10 points when it is not your turn, the game continues until any player (including you) has 10 points on their turn.”
- Almanac, Ending the Game (p.7) and Victory Points (p.14) reiterate this.

Common house rules: Some groups play simultaneous reveals at game end and then break ties (e.g., most cities, resources, etc.), but that is not in the official rules.

scores.reasoning: The generated answer perfectly explains that ties are not possible because victory can only be declared on a player's own turn, aligning completely with the gold standard and official rules. It is also very concise.

answers.question: When discarding cards for the Robber, do development cards count toward hand size?
answers.response:
No. Only resource cards count toward the hand size for discarding when a 7 is rolled.

- Official rule: “every player who has more than 7 resource cards must select half (rounded down) of their resource cards and return them to the bank” (Game Rules, p.5, 4.a “Rolling a ‘7’ and Activating the Robber”). The Almanac repeats: “Each player counts their resource cards… Any player with more than 7 resource cards… must discard” (Almanac, p.11).
- Development cards are not resource cards, so they are not counted and are never discarded for this effect.

This is the official rule; house rules sometimes count all cards, but that is nonstandard.

answers.question: Can new settlements be built along existing roads?
answers.response:
Yes—if the intersection is legal and connected to your road.

- Official rule: A new settlement may be built “only on an unoccupied intersection” and “must always connect to 1 or more of your own roads,” while observing the Distance Rule (no settlement/city on any of the 3 adjacent intersections) (Almanac: Settlements, p. 11; Distance Rule, p. 7).
- Roads run along paths (edges), and intersections can have roads leading in; you may place a settlement at such an intersection if it’s vacant and you have a road into it. This can even break an opponent’s road network if they only have roads there (Almanac: Longest Road, example on p. 9).

Clarification:
- During initial setup you place a settlement then an adjacent road (Almanac: Set-Up Phase, p. 12). During the game, you must have your road in place first to build the settlement (Settlements, p. 11).

scores.reasoning: The generated answer is perfectly correct and very complete, providing more detail and context than the gold answer, including rule citations and a clarification about setup. However, its extensive detail makes it less concise than the gold answer, which directly answers the question with only the essential conditions.

answers.question: What happens when the birds turmoil for the 4th time?
answers.response:
You just pick a new leader again—there’s no extra penalty or loss.

- In Turmoil, you Depose your current leader and must choose a new one from those face up (7.7.3).
- If this is the 4th Turmoil and all four leaders are now face down, “A New Clutch” applies: flip all leaders face up and choose any one (7.7.3.I).
- Then proceed to Rest (end Daylight and begin Evening) as normal (7.7.4).

Official rulebook refs: 7.7, 7.7.3, 7.7.3.I.

answers.question: What are the types of Corvid plots?
answers.response:
Bomb, Snare, Extortion, and Raid.

Reference: The Corvid Conspiracy has eight plot tokens—two of each type (13.2.2). Their types and flip/remove effects are detailed in 13.7.1–13.7.4:
- Bomb: on flip, remove all enemy pieces in the clearing; then remove the bomb.
- Snare: face up, enemies can’t place pieces in or move pieces from the clearing.
- Extortion: on flip, take a random card from each enemy with pieces there; face up, +1 card draw in Evening.
- Raid: when removed (face up or down), place one Corvid warrior in each adjacent clearing (ignored if removed by Exposure).

answers.question: What is the difference between buildings, tokens, and units?
answers.response:
Direct answer:
- Buildings = square cardboard pieces owned by a faction/hireling. They occupy building slots in clearings and count toward rule. Removing an enemy building scores 1 VP. See Glossary G.3; Slots 2.2.3; Rule 2.5; Scoring 3.2.1.
- Tokens = round cardboard pieces owned by a faction/hireling (e.g., sympathy, wood, plots, mobs). They do not occupy slots (unless a rule says so), do not count toward rule, and removing an enemy token scores 1 VP. See Glossary G.34; Rule 2.5; Scoring 3.2.1.
- “Units” (not a formal term in the Law) = the wooden figures: warriors and pawns. Warriors are wooden figures that move, battle, and count toward rule; pawns are wooden figures that move and battle but do not count toward rule. See Glossary G.36 (Warrior) and G.18 (Pawn); Rule 2.5 (only warriors and buildings count).

Notes on usage and effects (official rules):
- Counting rule: A player rules a clearing if they have more total warriors and buildings there than each other player; tokens and pawns do not contribute (2.5).
- Scoring for removal: You score when you remove enemy buildings or tokens, not when you remove enemy warriors (3.2.1).
- Piece ownership and limits: All of the above are faction/hireling pieces and are limited by physical components (1.5.1–1.5.3).

answers.question: If I strike another faction piece as the Vagabond, does that make my relationship hostile with that faction?
answers.response:
Short answer: Only if you remove a warrior.

- By rule 9.2.9.III, the Vagabond becomes Hostile with a faction “if you remove a warrior of a non‑Hostile faction,” regardless of how it was removed.
- The Strike action (9.5.6) lets you remove an enemy warrior; if you do, that faction immediately becomes Hostile. If there are no warriors and you instead remove a building or token with Strike, this does not change the relationship.
- Note: Strike is not a battle, so you do not score Infamy for pieces removed with Strike (9.2.9.IIIa applies only “in battle during your turn”). After a faction becomes Hostile, moving into any of their warriors’ clearings costs an extra boot (9.2.9.IIIb).

answers.question: When can an ambush card be used?
answers.response:
Root — Ambush timing and use

- When: Only in battle, at Step 1: Defender May Ambush (4.3.1).
- Who and how:
  - Defender may play exactly one ambush card that matches the clearing of battle (4.3.1).
    - If it resolves, the defender deals 2 hits immediately, then discards the card; if no attacking warriors/pawns remain, the battle ends (4.3.1.II).
  - Attacker may respond by playing an ambush card matching the clearing; this cancels the defender’s ambush (defender’s card is discarded and no ambush hits occur) (4.3.1.I).
  - The attacker never “ambushes” first; they can only foil the defender’s ambush.

- Card notes:
  - Ambush cards cannot be crafted (2.1.2).
  - A bird-suit ambush can be used in any clearing as a matching ambush; this is the standard/commonly accepted interpretation and is reflected in official play materials (2.1.2; Birds Are Wild, 2.1.1).

Summary citations: 2.1.2 (Ambush cards), 4.3.1 (Defender May Ambush and foil), 4.3.1.II (resolve hits), 1.4.2 (no interruptions outside allowed timing).

scores.reasoning: The generated answer is perfectly accurate, covering all points in the gold answer and adding valuable, correct details like the 'matching clearing' requirement and the specific rules for bird-suit ambushes. It is very complete. While slightly more detailed than the gold, the additional information is highly relevant and well-organized, making it still concise and practical for mid-game use.

answers.question: How does alliance revolt interact with the Lost City landmark?
answers.response:
Short answer
At Lost City, the Woodland Alliance treats the clearing as any one suit of its choice for the Revolt. So:
- You may place any one base (mouse, rabbit, or fox) there. (8.4.1.I)
- To Revolt there, you must spend two supporters of a single suit you choose for that Revolt (mouse/rabbit/fox; birds may substitute) rather than mixing suits. (8.4.1.II; “matching” means same suit, G.16.)
- After placing the base, you place warriors equal to the number of sympathetic clearings of the chosen suit; the Lost City clearing itself counts if it is sympathetic. (8.4.1.III)

Why
Lost City’s clearing is considered to have all three suits; whenever a rule asks for a clearing “matching a suit,” you pick one suit to use for that effect. The Law supports this interpretation in its treatment of multi‑suit clearings (see the Lizard Cult’s note about Lost City and suit matching, 10.2.1) and by the glossary definition of “matching” (G.16).

Status of rulings
- Official rules: Revolt steps (8.4.1); “matching” means same suit (G.16); Lost City creates multi‑suit interactions (10.2.1 note).
- Common clarification (widely accepted): In a multi‑suit clearing such as Lost City, you choose one suit for the action and all “matching” cards you spend must share that suit.

answers.question: Which factions can lose Victory Points?
answers.response:
Short answer: Only two cases in the Law let a faction lose victory points.

- Eyrie Dynasties: When they fall into Turmoil, Step 1 Humiliate says “Lose one victory point per bird card (including Loyal Viziers) on the Decree.” (7.7.1)

- Vagabond (Jailor character only): The Jailor’s special action Coerce says “After the action that it prompts or adds to, you lose 1 victory point.” (V.12.2)

Notes
- No other faction has a rule that causes VP loss in this Law. Other setbacks (discarding cards, losing pieces, etc.) don’t reduce VP.

answers.question: Can the Riverfolk Company use the Tunnels card to move between trade posts?
answers.response:
No. Tunnels connects clearings with your buildings. Riverfolk trade posts are tokens, not buildings, so they don’t qualify. 

- Trade posts are explicitly tokens (11.2.5; Glossary G.34), not buildings (G.3).  
- The Tunnels card (persistent effect) specifies movement between clearings with your buildings. 

Therefore, the Riverfolk Company cannot use Tunnels to move between their trade posts.

answers.question: Can I purchase mercenaries and use them for the dominance victory condition on the same turn?
answers.response:
No.

- Rule: Riverfolk Mercenaries explicitly “cannot be counted toward dominance” by the buyer (11.2.7 III).
- Timing: Dominance cards check “at the start of your Birdsong” (3.3.1). Buying services also happens “at the start of [your] Birdsong” (11.2.6), and while you may choose the order (1.1.3), Mercenaries only apply for Daylight and Evening of that turn and still cannot count for dominance (11.2.7 III).

Therefore you cannot purchase Mercenaries and use them to satisfy a dominance victory on the same turn—or any turn. This is an official rule, not a house rule.

answers.question: As the Scoundrel, can I Scorched Earth the clearing with the Marquise' Keep?
answers.response:
No. You cannot use Scorched Earth in the Keep clearing.

- Why: The Keep rule (6.2.2) says “other players cannot [place] pieces in the clearing with the keep token.” Scorched Earth (V.6.2) requires you to “exhaust a F and place it in your clearing” to remove all enemy pieces. Items are pieces (G.13, G.19). So placing the Torch item in the Keep clearing is prohibited.
- “Cannot” is absolute unless explicitly overridden (1.1.2), and Scorched Earth does not override the Keep.

Official ruling: You cannot perform Scorched Earth there while the Keep is present. If the Keep has been removed by some other effect, then you could. (House rules sometimes allow it, but that is not the official rule.)

answers.question: If a player qualifies for multiple nobles, can they take all of them on their turn?
answers.response: No. You may only acquire 1 Noble tile per turn. If you meet the requirements for several nobles, you must choose one. This check happens at the end of your turn and is not an action (rulebook, Noble Tiles section: “You may only acquire 1 Noble tile per turn … If you meet the swaying requirements for several Noble tiles, you must choose 1”).

answers.question: How do you win a noble?
answers.response:
You gain a Noble at the end of your turn if you meet that Noble’s swaying requirement using permanent bonuses from your Development cards.

- Check at the end of your turn (not an action). If you have the number and colors of bonuses shown on a Noble tile, take that tile and keep it for its Prestige.  
- Only bonuses count—Gem pieces do not.  
- You may take at most 1 Noble per turn; if you qualify for several, choose one.

Source: Rulebook, “Noble Tiles” section (“At the end of your turn… you must have the number and color of bonuses indicated on the tile… You may only acquire 1 Noble tile per turn.”)

answers.question: Can I take 2 gems of the same color if there are only 3 left?
answers.response:
No. You can only take 2 gems of the same color if there are at least 4 of that color in the supply before you take them. With only 3 left, the action is not allowed.

Official rule: Actions — “Take 2 Gem pieces of the same color: This action is only available if there are at least 4 pieces available in that color in the supply before you take the 2 pieces. You can never take Gold pieces with this action.” (Splendor rulebook, Actions section)

Notes:
- This applies in all player counts. In a 2‑player game (where each color starts with 4 gems), you can only do this when that color is still at 4. If it has dropped to 3, you cannot take 2.

40 tokens total.

answers.question: Which cards can I buy on my turn?
answers.response:
You can buy exactly one Development card on your turn, and it must be either:
- Any face‑up Development card in the tableau (the 4 cards from any level), or
- One of your own reserved cards.

You cannot buy a face‑down card directly from a deck, and Noble tiles are not purchased (they are claimed at the end of your turn if you meet their bonus requirements).

Rules reference: “Actions — Purchase 1 Development card” (Game Overview). You must pay the card’s cost after applying your permanent discounts from previously acquired card bonuses; Gold pieces may be used as wilds.

answers.question: If I cannot take 3 different colored gem tokens because there are only 2 colors left, each with less than 4 in stock, am I allowed to take just 2?
answers.response: Yes. Under the action “Take 3 Gem pieces of different colors,” the rules say: “If there are not enough available pieces to take 3 of different colors from the supply, you may take 2 pieces or even 1.” This does not require “at least 4 in stock”—that restriction only applies to the separate action “Take 2 Gem pieces of the same color.” Note you still must take different colors (no Gold) when using the “3 different” action.

answers.question: Can I spend 4 gold tokens to buy a card that costs 4 gems?
answers.response:
Yes. You may pay an entire card cost with gold.

Rule reference: Actions – “Purchase 1 Development card”: “The spent pieces (including any Gold pieces) are returned to the supply. Gold pieces are wild and can be used in place of any Gem, even if you have a Gem of that color.” There’s no limit on how many gold pieces you can use, so 4 gold can buy a card that costs 4 gems. (Gold only pays costs; it doesn’t count as a permanent bonus or toward nobles.)

answers.question: Do reserved cards need to be visible to all other players at the table?
answers.response:
No. Reserved cards are hidden information.

- Official rules: “Reserved cards are kept in your hand” and when reserving from a deck you “draw the top card… without showing it to the other players.” (Actions – Reserve 1 Development card). Only your pieces must be visible: “Your pieces must be visible to all players at all times.” (Piece pool limit). There’s no requirement to display reserved cards.
- Clarification: If you reserve a face-up card from the display, everyone saw what it was at the moment you took it, but you don’t have to keep it visible afterward.
- Common house rule: Some groups place reserved cards face up in front of players for convenience; this is not required by the official rules and reveals extra information.

answers.question: What happens when a player cannot do any of the three required actions on their turn?
answers.response:
You can’t pass. Per Actions: “On your turn, you must perform exactly 1 of these four actions.” If you can’t take gems or buy, you must use the reserve action—take a face‑up card or draw one from a deck; if there’s no Gold left, you still reserve the card but don’t take Gold (Actions: “If no Gold pieces remain in the supply, you may still reserve a card.”).

Note: The only time you couldn’t do this is if you already have 3 reserved cards; however, in normal play you can still perform an action by taking at least 1 gem via “Take 3 Gem pieces of different colors” (which allows taking 2 or even 1 if that’s all that’s available). The rules provide no option to pass.

answers.question: When purchasing cards, can I choose to pay with gem tokens instead of cards or vice versa?
answers.response:
No. Your development-card bonuses automatically reduce the cost; you cannot choose to “pay with tokens instead of bonuses,” nor can you overpay.

Relevant rules:
- “Development cards you acquire have a bonus… Each such bonus provides a discount of 1 Gem matching the color of the card.” And “If you have enough bonuses… you can even purchase a card without spending any pieces.” (Bonuses section, Game Overview)
- When you “Purchase 1 Development card,” you then pay the remaining cost with gem tokens and/or Gold; Gold is a wild token (“can be used in place of any Gem”). You return only the pieces actually required after discounts.

Practical examples:
- If a card costs 3 blue and you have 2 blue bonuses, you must pay exactly 1 blue (or 1 Gold) token—no option to pay 3 blue tokens.
- If your bonuses fully cover the cost, the card is free; you may not choose to spend tokens anyway.

This is the official rule as written; it’s also the common ruling used in play.
